coredata.dc:title: Robust carbon dots-wrapped NiCo flower-like nanostructures for enhanced triiodide reduction
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Nam Anh, Tran
Hai Linh, Dang Thi
Hai Linh, Nguyen
Thi Hien, Nguyen
Thi Hanh, Nguyen
Hung Vu, Ngoc
Hong Ha, Vu Thi
Ha Giang, Tran Thi
Van Tan, Tran
Dao, Van-Duong
coredata.dc:description: Replacing traditional counter electrodes (CEs) with Pt-free electrodes is one of the challenges for developing dye-sensitized solar cells (DSCs). Herein, we successfully synthesized carbon dots-wrapped NiCo flower-like nanostructure (NiCo/Cdots) and evaluated its application as CEs for DSCs. To characterize as-prepared materials, we conducted FESEM and TEM-EDS. Cyclic Voltammetry (CV), electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS), Bode, and Tafel plots were then used to investigate the electrocatalytic properties of NiCo/Cdots CE. As a result, the as-prepared sample exhibited high electrocatalytic activity, low charger-transfer resistance, and large exchange current density. DSC based on NiCo/Cdots CE was assembled and showed a slightly lower PCE than Pt CE-based DSCs (8.27 and 8.52 %, respectively). However, it provided excellent stability during a long-term performance. These results indicated that CoNi/Cdots is a low-cost, easy-to-fabricate material with high potential as a CE of DSCs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: NiCo flower
Nanohybrid
Cdots
Counter electrode
Dye-sensitized solar cells

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) FESEM, (b, c, d) TEM, (e, f, g, h) Elemental mapping images of NiCo/Cdots.
A) cv curves, b) nyquist plots, c) bode plots, and d) tafel curves of nico/cdots and pt-based dsc.

(a) Photocurrent density–voltage curves of DSC with NiCo/Cdots and Pt as CEs under standard conditions, (b) IPCE curves of distinct DSCs, Time-resolved photocurrent responses during (c) light on–off cycles, and (d) a prolonged experiment.
Measured electrochemical parameters from CV analysis of NiCo/Cdots and Pt CE samples.
Measured electrochemical parameters from EIS analysis of NiCo/Cdots and Pt CE samples.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The PCE value of DSCs based on NiCo/Carbon CEs.

row.entry: Co nanoparticles embedded graphitic ordered mesoporous carbon

head.ce:title: Robust carbon dots-wrapped NiCo flower-like nanostructures for enhanced triiodide reduction

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Biotechnology, Chemistry and Environmental Engineering, PHENIKAA University, Hanoi 10000, Viet Nam

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Biotechnology, Chemistry and Environmental Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Biotechnology, Chemistry and Environmental Engineering, PHENIKAA University, Hanoi 10000, Viet Nam

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Environmental Sciences, University of Science, Vietnam National University, 334 Nguyen Trai, Hanoi 10000, Viet Nam
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Environmental Sciences, University of Science, Vietnam National University, 334 Nguyen Trai, Hanoi 10000, Viet Nam

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Replacing traditional counter electrodes (CEs) with Pt-free electrodes is one of the challenges for developing dye-sensitized solar cells (DSCs). Herein, we successfully synthesized carbon dots-wrapped NiCo flower-like nanostructure (NiCo/Cdots) and evaluated its application as CEs for DSCs. To characterize as-prepared materials, we conducted FESEM and TEM-EDS. Cyclic Voltammetry (CV), electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS), Bode, and Tafel plots were then used to investigate the electrocatalytic properties of NiCo/Cdots CE. As a result, the as-prepared sample exhibited high electrocatalytic activity, low charger-transfer resistance, and large exchange current density. DSC based on NiCo/Cdots CE was assembled and showed a slightly lower PCE than Pt CE-based DSCs (8.27 and 8.52 %, respectively). However, it provided excellent stability during a long-term performance. These results indicated that CoNi/Cdots is a low-cost, easy-to-fabricate material with high potential as a CE of DSCs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The next-generation photovoltaic cells, including quantum dots and organic–inorganic hybrid perovskite solar cells [1–3], have received a lot of attraction due to their low cost, easy fabrication, and high-power conversion efficiencies (PCEs). However, third-generation solar cells, known as dye-sensitized solar cells (DSCs), are still receiving much attention due to their low cost, ease of production, adjustable color and transparency, flexibility, and environmental friendliness [4–8]. Normally, DSCs are fabricated with four main components, including a mesoporous TiO2 nanocrystal working electrode, ruthenium-based dyes (Ru), I−/I3 − electrolyte, and a Pt counter electrode (CE).[9] It can not be denied that platinum is an ideal material for the fabrication of CEs, which contributes to the operating efficiency of the DSCs. Specifically, Pt CE can gather electrons from the external circuit and boost the regeneration of dyes by catalyzing the I−/I3 − redox reaction to take place quickly, continuously, and persistently [10]. However, due to certain constraints, such as high cost, limited reserves in nature, and the possibility of decomposition to form PtI4 in the electrolyte, Pt is not suitable for commercial DSCs with long-term performance. In other words, finding cheaper alternative materials for CE is one of the main challenges in the development of DSC. In 1996, the first Pt-free-based DSCs were synthesized by Kay and Gratzel, using graphite and carbon black-based CEs.The corresponding cell achieved an overall efficiency of 6.7 % [11]. Up to now, several groups of materials are expected to replace Pt CE such as carbon materials (CNT, graphene), polymers (PEDOT, PANI), transition metal oxides, and sulfides (MoS2), perovskite, etc. [12–22]. For example, Wen Xi et al. used graphene-supported small-sized FeSe2 (FeSe2/G) as CE materials for DSCs. The FeSe2/G composites-based DSC showed a remarkable photovoltaic performance of 8.69 %, higher than traditional Pt-based devices (7.70 %) [23]. Ni12P5/N, S-doped graphene-based CE also exhibited excellent catalytic activity, resulting in a great PCE of DSC which is comparable to that of Pt-based DSC [24]. It can be seen that metal–carbon hybrid materials have recently become a promising group for Pt-free CEs. Firstly, carbon materials not only have a stable structure with a large surface area, and good catalytic activity but also are inexpensive, environmentally friendly, and corrosion-resistant. Moreover, metal nanoparticles exhibit high electrical conductivity, excellent electrochemical properties, and good mechanical strength. Note that to take advantage of each material group, the structure of hybrid materials, which are used as CEs of DSCs, usually contains metal as a core and carbon nanoparticles as the outer layer. For example, the DSC utilizing NiCu bimetallic nanoparticle-decorated graphene CE exhibited a higher PCE compared to the pristine graphene, and Ni-based cells [25]. DSCs with NiCu nanoparticle-doped carbon nanofibers and Pt CEs showed PCE of 3.5 and 5.6 %, respectively [26]. CoNi/carbonaceous nanohybrids have also been studied as CE materials and evaluated their efficiencies.[27–29] It is well-known that NiCo has shown excellent physical, catalytic, and magnetic properties.[29] Tang et al. synthesized Co–Ni alloy by a simple hydrothermal method, and used them as CE materials [30]. As a result, the CoNi, Co, and Ni counter electrodes reached PCE of 1.51, 1.27, and 0.54 %. The efficiency of the device with NiCo CE achieved 0.03 % in the research of Dao et al, much lower than that of Pt CE-based DSCs (6.63 %) [31].

Recently, carbon dots have been synthesized and applied in many fields, especially energy storage and conversion, catalysis, etc. [32–36]. Carbon (graphene dot, Cdot)-wrapped Pd, Ni, and PtMo materials were synthesized and provided superior stability with long-term performance electrocatalysts for hydrogen evolution reactions in highly acidic media [37–39]. This is due to the formation of a protective Cdots layer. Besides, carbon dots also provide more defect and active sites, promoting ion diffusion and electron transport [40–42]. Furthermore, doping carbon dots enhances electron acceptance and donation of counter-electrode materials [43,44].

Herein, the bimetallic NiCo flower-like nanoparticles are wrapped by carbon dots (NiCo/Cdots) to form a uniform and robust structure for the first time, which will be applied as Pt-free CE of DSC. Cdots are expected to enhance the active sites, corrosion resistance, electrical conductivity as well as the overall catalytic activity of the as-prepared material.[45,46] As a result, the PCE of the DSC based on NiCo/Cdots was 8.27 %, which was comparable to the Pt CE-based DSC (8.52 %) under standard conditions. The findings in this work contributes to the development of Pt-free CEs, and in general third-generation photovoltaic cells.

NiCo was synthesized by a template-free hydrothermal method as the procedure in previous research [47]. Briefly, cobalt chloride hexahydrate (CoCl2·6H2O, 0.625 g) and nickel chloride hexahydrate (NiCl2·6H2O, 0.624 g) were completely dissolved in 25 ml of deionized water by magnetic stirring for 20 min. Besides, the sodium hydroxide solution was separately prepared by dissolving NaOH (1.05 g) in 10 ml of deionized water. Subsequently, the NaOH solution was added dropwise to the previously prepared metal salt solution. This mixture was stirred continuously for 20 min to achieve a homogeneous state. After that, 10 ml of hydrazine hydrate 85 % solution (N2H4·H2O) was added and continued to stir for 20 min. The obtained solution was poured into a Teflon cup to prepare for the hydrothermal process. This process is carried out at 120 °C for 16 h. The solution was then allowed to cool to room temperature and centrifuged to obtain NiCo particles. Finally, they were rinsed with deionized water to remove impurities and dried in an oven at 45 °C overnight.

The synthesis process of Cdots by the microwave method was described in our previous report.[48] Specifically, citric acid (C₆H₈O₇, 3 g) and urea (CH₄N₂O, 3 g) were completely dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water to form a transparent solution. This solution was carbonized in a domestic microwave oven (700 W) for 4 min. After the carbonization process and cooling down to room temperature, the obtained solution was filtered through a Whatman filter paper to remove any insoluble parts before using and storing.

First, the NiCo solution was prepared by dispersing as-prepare bimetallic nanoparticles in deionized water. After that, Cdots solution was added to NiCo solution at room temperature. The obtained mixture is left to stand for 1 h. Finally, NiCo/Cdot particles were collected by centrifugation, rinsed with deionized water, and dried at 60 °C.

The Nafion solution is prepared firstly from 0.04 ml Nafion in 100 ml isopropyl alcohol. Note the volume ratio of isopropyl alcohol in water is 80:20. Next, 2.0 mg NiCo/Cdots is dispersed in Nafion solution by blending. 5 μL of the catalyst ink was deposited onto the FTO glass substrate. The NiCo/Cdots CEs were archived after drying at oven 70 °C/30 min. The Pt electrode was prepared as the process in our previous work.3

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the working electrode, assembly, and evaluation of DSCs
ce:section.ce:para: The fabrication procedure of working electrode, assembly, and evaluation of DSCs followed our previous research, which was further described in Supporting Information [49].

Field emission Scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) was first used to check the morphology of NiCo/Cdots particles. CoNi/Cdots nanoparticles exhibit a uniform and 3D flower-like structure with average diameters of about 2.08 µm (Fig. 1 (a)). Transmission electron microscopy-Elemental Mapping (TEM-mapping) was then employed to further access the components and element distribution. Fig. 1((d)−(g)) shows the nanorods with a length in the range of 150–200 nm, which are formed from Co (green points) and Ni (yellow points) atoms. According to Pan et al., these nanorods are the main units to form flower-like nanoparticles.[47] The high magnification TEM-mapping images of CoNi/Cdots (Fig. 1(c), (h)) reveal the existence of Cdots, which have a uniform spherical morphology with an average size of 4.9 nm. Furthermore, these Cdots wrapped around the CoNi rod to form a thin layer with a thickness of about 12 nm. Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy was conducted to reconfirm the existence of Cdots on the superficies of NiCo alloy. As shown in Fig. S1, it can be easily found that the peak of Cdot appears at about 0.28 keV.

To evaluate and compare the electrocatalytic properties of NiCo/Cdots CE and traditional platinum electrode, several analytical techniques, namely cyclic voltammetry (CV), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), Bode, and Tafel plots, were employed. As the results of CV (Fig. 2 (a) and Table 1 ), one pair of redox peaks was found for both CEs. In the case of NiCo/Cdots, the redox peaks appeared at 0.002 and 0.4 V, and the discrepancy in the separation between the two peak potentials (ΔE) was 0.398 V. The redox peaks of Pt emerged at 0 and 0.42 V, leading to a ΔE of 0.42 V. Note that ΔE value and the charge transfer rate are negatively correlated.[50,51] The peaks at more positive and more negative potentials represent reactions (1) and (2), respectively:
(1)
3
I -
- 2
e -
→
I 3 -
(2)
I 3 -
+ 2
e -
→ 3
I -

First, the presence of a pair redox peak indicates that NiCo/Cdots CE has the electrocatalytic ability to reduce the triiodide ions. The second, the absolute reduction current (|Jred|) of NiCo/Cdots and Pt CE are 1.615 and 1.767 mA, respectively, which predicts that the efficiency of triiodide ions reduction on the NiCo/Cdots CE is lower than that of Pt CE. The third, ΔE values of both CEs suggest the lower open-circuit voltage (Voc) would be obtained from the NiCo/Cdots CE-based DSCs. Note that the Jred and ΔE values of NiCo/Cdots are only about 9 % lower than those of the conventional Pt electrode. Therefore, NiCo/Cdots can replace Pt due to an acceptable decline in the catalytic rate for I− regeneration compared to Pt.

EIS analysis was used to understand the charge transfer phenomena at the interface between the electrolyte and CE. The Nyquist plots for NiCo/Cdots and Pt CEs are shown in Fig. 2(b). It can be observed that there are two semicircles in both Nyquist curves. Among them, the first-semicircle, which is recorded in the high-frequency region, is characterized by the impedance of charge transfer occurring at the electrode and electrolyte interface. The first semicircle in the Nyquist plot of NiCo/Cdots CE is larger, meaning it has a higher charge transfer resistance (Rct). By fitting an equivalent circuit using the Z-view software, fitting parameters are calculated and listed in Table 2 . As a result, the obtained Rct of NiCo/Cdots (1.97 Ω) is higher than that of Pt CE (1.74 Ω). On the one hand, the obtained Rct values of both CEs are also consistent with the semicircle-based comment. On the other hand, these impedance values also demonstrate that NiCo/Cdots material has a lower electrocatalytic ability toward I−/I3 − redox couple than that of Pt. Moreover, the higher Rct value of NiCo/Cdots CE also indicates a decrease in the exchange current density and electron transport kinetics at the electrolyte/CE interface [48]. In addition, the ohmic internal resistance (Rh), which is affected by the substrates and CE, can be determined to be the value of the starting point of the first semicircle in the Z’. The higher Rh value shows a lower conductivity of the catalytic NiCo/Cdots CE [52]. Another parameter obtained from EIS, the constant phase element of NiCo/Cdots is slightly higher than that of Pt CE. Note that a larger CPE suggests a higher active surface area of the CE. A higher active surface area leads to faster diffusion of the redox couple in the I−/I3 − electrolyte. This remark is confirmed by the Warburg diffusion impedance value of both CEs. As a result, the series resistance (Rs) of DSC used NiCo/Cdots CE and Pt CE are calculated to be 7.74 and 7.23 Ω, respectively (Rs = Rh + Rct + Zw (R)).

Bode phase plot analysis was then used to clarify the charge-transfer kinetics of the electrode further. From the bode curve, the electron lifetime (τ) can be calculated by the equation τ = 1/(2πfmax), where fmax is the peak frequency corresponding to the charger transfer process at the counter electrode/electrolyte interface. As shown in Fig. 2(c), the fmax value of NiCo/Cdots is lower than Pt CE. As a result, the calculated electron lifetime of NiCo/Cdots- and Pt-based DSCs are 96.62 and 39.99 µs, respectively. Noticeably, a shorter lifetime denotes the rapid electron transfer rate and faster reduction kinetics of I3 − ions, leading to the higher electrocatalytic ability of the CEs [53].

The electrochemical activity and diffusion ability of the counter electrodes are expressed through the exchange current density (Jo) and diffusion-limited current density (Jlim) parameters, which are determined through the Tafel polarization curves. The exchange current density (J0) directly denotes electron transfer kinetics under equilibrium conditions at the CE/electrolyte contact [54]. Meanwhile, the diffusion-limited current density Jlim value is related to the diffusion coefficient of the I3 –/I– pair in the electrolyte [55]. As shown in Fig. 2(d), J0 and Jlim of Pt CE are slightly higher, denoting that NiCo/Cdots CE have a lower catalytic activity. These results are well-assorted with previous discussions. Besides, a lower diffusion-limited current density also predicts a decrease in the photovoltaic performance of the NiCo/Cdots-based DSC [56].

The photocurrent density–voltage (J-V) and incident photon to current efficiency (IPCE) analysis were measured to evaluate the effect of different CEs on DSC’s performance (Fig. 3 (a) and (b), respectively) and the obtained results are presented in Table 3 . NiCo/Cdots-based cell showed the peak photoelectric response at 540 nm, corresponding to an IPCE value of 75.65 %. The current density versus voltage curve of DSC with NiCo/Cdots CE exhibits an open-circuit voltage (VOC) of 760 mV, a short-circuit current density (JSC) of 15.8 mAcm−2, and a fill factor (FF) of 68.35 %, leading a power conversion efficiency (PCE) of 8.27 %. In contrast, the DSC employing the Pt CE shows a higher VOC of 775 mV, JSC of 15.8 mAcm−2, FF of 69.62 %, and PCE of 8.52 %. Overall, these photovoltaic parameters of both DSCs are in good agreement with previous results. It is noteworthy that despite completely replacing the precious metal (Pt) with a cheap, easy-to-fabricate, and environment-friendly material, DSC using NiCo/Cdots CE still achieves a high PCE, equal to 96.27 % compared to that of Pt CE-based DSCs. Other studies on NiCo/Carbon-based C of DSCs are summarized in Table 4 .

To evaluate the practical applicability of DSCs, fast response, multiple light on–off cycles, and stability are important parameters. Thus, this study analyzed the photocurrent response by the time under the light of a solar simulator. As shown in Fig. 3(c), DSC using NiCo/Cdots CE immediately generated photocurrent density when the light suddenly turned on, and conversely, it disappeared as soon as the light turned off. The rapid photocurrent response of NiCo/Cdots CE-based DSC is evidenced by its remarkably short delay (less than 1 s) between the generation and disappearance of the photocurrent density. Moreover, DSC using NiCo/Cdot CE still shows a good response with a stable photocurrent density after four light on–off cycles. It shows the suitability of NiCo/Cdots CE as a substitute for Pt CE in practice. Besides, the result obtained from the stability test of NiCo/Cdots-based DSC is shown in Fig. 3(d). It was clearly observed that the photocurrent density was unchanged during the entire test period. In other words, NiCo/Cdots CE can easily allow electrolyte diffusion and electron transport, as well as stable operation under redox reaction conditions.

In this work, the robust carbon dot-wrapped NiCo flower-like nanoparticles were fabricated by a fast, facile, and inexpensive route. With the Cdots-based shell in a thickness of 12 nm, NiCo/Cdots CE showed high electrocatalytic activity for the redox reactions of the electrolyte and good stability in a harsh environment. Under standard conditions, the PCE value of the cell using NiCo/Cdots CE was slightly lower than that of DSC using traditional platinum electrode (8.27 and 8.52 %, respectively). Moreover, DSC based on NiCo/Cdots CE also showed stable performance over time and light on–off cycles. In summary, NiCo/Cdots is one of the ideal materials to replace traditional Pt in DSCs, increasing the practical applicability of liquid junction PV devices.

Tran Nam Anh: Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Dang Thi Hai Linh: Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Nguyen Hai Linh: Data curation, Formal analysis. Nguyen Thi Hien: Data curation, Formal analysis. Nguyen Thi Hanh: Formal analysis. Ngoc Hung Vu: Formal analysis. Vu Thi Hong Ha: Formal analysis. Tran Thi Ha Giang: Formal analysis. Tran Van Tan: Formal analysis. Van-Duong Dao: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge the financial support from the Vietnam National Foundation for Science and Technology Development (NAFOSTED) under grant number 103.02-2021.106, Phenikaa Innovation Foundation grant (ĐMST.2022.02). Tran Nam Anh was funded by the Ph.D. Scholarship Programme of Vingroup Innovation Foundation (VINIF), code VINIF.2023.TS.006.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jelechem.2024.118441.

The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
